questions.prompt: Are you asking about your own record?
options: I am helping someone complete their own check

questions.prompt: Is this a state or local matter from Minnesota, rather than a federal matter?
How did the Minnesota case or record end?
options: Arrest or citation with no charge filed
Dismissed, no-billed, nolle prosequi, or not prosecuted
Diversion, deferred disposition, supervision, or similar program

questions.prompt: What level or type was the charge?
Do any of these Minnesota descriptions sound close to your situation?
options: Arrest-identification-data destruction when no charges were filed (Minn. Stat. § 299C.11)
Cannabis automatic or board-reviewed expungement under §§ 609A.055–.06
Prosecutor-agreed sealing without a full petition under § 609A.025
None of these / I am not sure

pathways.label: Arrest-identification-data destruction when no charges were filed (Minn. Stat. § 299C.11)
pathways.summary: Wilma should screen Minnesota cases through these pathways in order: Arrest but No Charges Filed If the person was arrested but no charges were filed, there may be no court case to expunge. The person may instead request destruction of arrest identification data such as fingerprints, photographs, and identification records under Minn. Stat. 299C.11, if they meet the statutory conditions. This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years.…
pathways.sourceRef: section:15-62; section:243-283; section:382-401; corpus-unit:17; corpus-unit:14; decision_tree:243-283; corpus-unit:113; corpus-unit:141
ruleClauses: Wilma should screen Minnesota cases through these pathways in order:
Arrest but No Charges Filed

If the person was arrested but no charges were filed, there may be no court case to expunge. The person may instead request destruction of arrest identification data such as fingerprints, photographs, and identification records under Minn. Stat. 299C.11, if they meet the statutory conditions. This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/299C.11")
Were charges ever filed in court, or was this only an arrest?
If only an arrest, route to non-court arrest-record relief, not the standard court expungement petition.

If the case was dismissed because of mistaken identity, Minnesota has an automatic expungement pathway under 609A.017. Mistaken identity can include identity theft, someone else using the persons identifying information, witness or law-enforcement misidentification, misinformation, or another mistake. If the prosecutor dismisses or moves to dismiss before conviction and states the dismissal is due to mistaken identity, the court must issue an expungement order without a petition. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.017")
Was the case dismissed because the court or prosecutor determined you were not the person involved?
Automatic Clean Slate Expungement

Minnesota has automatic expungement under 609A.015 for certain records. The BCA identifies potentially eligible cases, notifies the Judicial Branch, and qualifying records are sealed without the person filing a petition unless a court order prohibits sealing or the record is later determined ineligible. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Automatic expungement may apply to certain:
Record Type Basic Automatic Rule
Dismissed/resolved-in-favor cases Some cases qualify automatically after dismissal or favorable resolution.
Diversion or stay of adjudication Some nonfelony cases qualify after successful completion and at least 1 year without new charge/conviction, except petty misdemeanors.
Petty misdemeanor or misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 2 years from discharge.
Gross misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 3 years from discharge.
Certain felony convictions Some qualify after 4 or 5 years depending on offense type.

Automatic Clean Slate relief does not automatically seal records held by the Commissioners of Children, Youth, and Families; Health; or Human Services. People who need relief affecting those agencies may still need a petition. (
"https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Even if your case may qualify for automatic sealing, that may not fully solve background checks involving DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, teacher licensing, or certain government/licensing reviews.
Minnesota has special cannabis-related expungement rules.

Certain nonfelony cannabis records may be automatically expunged under 609A.055. The BCA identifies eligible records and seals them without an application, petition, or motion. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.055")

For felony and some other cannabis-related cases, Minnesota created the Cannabis Expungement Board. The Board reviews eligible cases, often anonymously, and determines whether the conviction should be expunged, vacated, dismissed, or resentenced based on changes in Minnesota cannabis law after August 1, 2023. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.06")

The Cannabis Expungement Board has stated that misdemeanor cannabis expungements may already have been processed automatically by the BCA, while felony and certain other cases are being reviewed over a multi-year period. ("https://mn.gov/ceb/impacted-individuals/")
Was the charge related to marijuana, cannabis flower, THC, or a controlled-substance offense that involved cannabis?
Prosecutor Agreement Without Petition

Under 609A.025, if the prosecutor agrees to sealing for a person eligible under 609A.02, subd. 3, the court may seal the record without a full petition process, unless public-safety interests outweigh the persons interests. The prosecutor must make a good-faith effort to notify victims and give them an opportunity to object. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.025")
Wilma should treat this as a possible attorney/prosecutor-negotiated route, not a default self-help route.
Petition-Based Expungement
waitingRules: If the person was arrested but no charges were filed, there may be no court case to expunge. The person may instead request destruction of arrest identification data such as fingerprints, photographs, and identification records under Minn. Stat. 299C.11, if they meet the statutory conditions. This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/299C.11")
Diversion or stay of adjudication Some nonfelony cases qualify after successful completion and at least 1 year without new charge/conviction, except petty misdemeanors.
Petty misdemeanor or misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 2 years from discharge.
Gross misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 3 years from discharge.
Certain felony convictions Some qualify after 4 or 5 years depending on offense type.
A person may petition after successful completion of diversion or a stay of adjudication if they were not charged with a new crime for at least 1 year after completion. (
Case Type Automatic Waiting Period
Petty misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Gross misdemeanor 3 years after discharge
Felony fifth-degree controlled substance under 152.025 4 years after discharge
Other qualifying felony 5 years after discharge
These are automatic Clean Slate waiting periods under 609A.015. The person must also have no disqualifying new conviction and must not be charged at the time eligibility is reviewed. (
Petition hearing timing At least 60 days after service; court guidance says process usually 4-6 months

If automatic expungement does not apply, has not happened yet, or does not cover the agencies the person needs, the person may file a petition under 609A.03, if eligible under 609A.02. Minnesota Courts specifically says a petition may be needed where the person wants relief beyond automatic expungement, including records involving DHS/DCYF. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")

Sex-Offense Registration Bar A conviction requiring registration under Minn. Stat. 243.166 is not eligible for expungement under the felony petition list. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.02")

The petitioner must serve the petition on required government agencies. Minnesota law also provides for victim notice in certain cases, and agencies may object or submit information. A hearing cannot be held sooner than 60 days after service; Minnesota Courts public guidance says the process usually takes at least 4 to 6 months, with the hearing scheduled at least 63 days after service. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")

Check whether 3 years have passed since discharge, no new conviction, no pending charges, and whether the offense is excluded.

243.166 is not eligible for expungement under the felony petition list. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.02") Wilma should escalate immediately if the user says: I had to register, sex offender registration, predatory offender registration, or anything similar.

A hearing cannot be held sooner than 60 days after service; Minnesota Courts public guidance says the process usually takes at least 4 to 6 months, with the hearing scheduled at least 63 days after service. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03") Step 5 - Hearing Agencies may object.

If Misdemeanor / Petty Misdemeanor Conviction Route to: Check whether 2 years have passed since discharge, no new conviction, no pending charges, and whether the offense is excluded from automatic relief.
If Gross Misdemeanor Conviction Route to: Check whether 3 years have passed since discharge, no new conviction, no pending charges, and whether the offense is excluded.

ruleClauses: Wilma should screen Minnesota cases through these pathways in order:
Arrest but No Charges Filed

If the person was arrested but no charges were filed, there may be no court case to expunge. The person may instead request destruction of arrest identification data such as fingerprints, photographs, and identification records under Minn. Stat. 299C.11, if they meet the statutory conditions. This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/299C.11")
Were charges ever filed in court, or was this only an arrest?
If only an arrest, route to non-court arrest-record relief, not the standard court expungement petition.

If the case was dismissed because of mistaken identity, Minnesota has an automatic expungement pathway under 609A.017. Mistaken identity can include identity theft, someone else using the persons identifying information, witness or law-enforcement misidentification, misinformation, or another mistake. If the prosecutor dismisses or moves to dismiss before conviction and states the dismissal is due to mistaken identity, the court must issue an expungement order without a petition. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.017")
Was the case dismissed because the court or prosecutor determined you were not the person involved?
Automatic Clean Slate Expungement

Minnesota has automatic expungement under 609A.015 for certain records. The BCA identifies potentially eligible cases, notifies the Judicial Branch, and qualifying records are sealed without the person filing a petition unless a court order prohibits sealing or the record is later determined ineligible. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Automatic expungement may apply to certain:
Record Type Basic Automatic Rule
Dismissed/resolved-in-favor cases Some cases qualify automatically after dismissal or favorable resolution.
Diversion or stay of adjudication Some nonfelony cases qualify after successful completion and at least 1 year without new charge/conviction, except petty misdemeanors.
Petty misdemeanor or misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 2 years from discharge.
Gross misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 3 years from discharge.
Certain felony convictions Some qualify after 4 or 5 years depending on offense type.

Automatic Clean Slate relief does not automatically seal records held by the Commissioners of Children, Youth, and Families; Health; or Human Services. People who need relief affecting those agencies may still need a petition. (
"https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Even if your case may qualify for automatic sealing, that may not fully solve background checks involving DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, teacher licensing, or certain government/licensing reviews.
Minnesota has special cannabis-related expungement rules.

Certain nonfelony cannabis records may be automatically expunged under 609A.055. The BCA identifies eligible records and seals them without an application, petition, or motion. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.055")

For felony and some other cannabis-related cases, Minnesota created the Cannabis Expungement Board. The Board reviews eligible cases, often anonymously, and determines whether the conviction should be expunged, vacated, dismissed, or resentenced based on changes in Minnesota cannabis law after August 1, 2023. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.06")

The Cannabis Expungement Board has stated that misdemeanor cannabis expungements may already have been processed automatically by the BCA, while felony and certain other cases are being reviewed over a multi-year period. ("https://mn.gov/ceb/impacted-individuals/")
Was the charge related to marijuana, cannabis flower, THC, or a controlled-substance offense that involved cannabis?
Prosecutor Agreement Without Petition

Under 609A.025, if the prosecutor agrees to sealing for a person eligible under 609A.02, subd. 3, the court may seal the record without a full petition process, unless public-safety interests outweigh the persons interests. The prosecutor must make a good-faith effort to notify victims and give them an opportunity to object. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.025")
Wilma should treat this as a possible attorney/prosecutor-negotiated route, not a default self-help route.
Petition-Based Expungement

This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/299C.11")

Petty misdemeanor or misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 2 years from discharge.
Gross misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 3 years from discharge.
Certain felony convictions Some qualify after 4 or 5 years depending on offense type.
A person may petition after successful completion of diversion or a stay of adjudication if they were not charged with a new crime for at least 1 year after completion. (
Case Type Automatic Waiting Period
Petty misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Gross misdemeanor 3 years after discharge
Felony fifth-degree controlled substance under 152.025 4 years after discharge
Other qualifying felony 5 years after discharge
These are automatic Clean Slate waiting periods under 609A.015. The person must also have no disqualifying new conviction and must not be charged at the time eligibility is reviewed. (
Petition hearing timing At least 60 days after service; court guidance says process usually 4-6 months

Expungement does not destroy records. It may not seal DNA records in certain cases. Criminal justice agencies may access or exchange expunged records for investigation, prosecution, sentencing, probation, and correctional-services purposes. Certain conviction records may also be opened for criminal-justice employment, DHS background studies, and teacher licensing unless the order specifically addresses those agencies where required. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")
An expungement order can be powerful, but it is not the same as deleting the record forever. Some agencies may still access sealed records for specific legal reasons.
Private Background Check Companies

Minnesota expungement orders bind courts and agencies, but private background-check companies may already have old data. Wilma should advise users to keep certified copies of orders and dispute inaccurate private background reports after expungement.

sourceSections.title: Core Rule: What Minnesota Expungement Means
sourceSections.text:
In Minnesota, expungement generally means sealing criminal records from public view. It does not destroy the record. Courts, law enforcement, prosecutors, the BCA, and some agencies may still access or reopen sealed records in legally permitted circumstances. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement")
Wilma should say:

Minnesota expungement usually seals the record from public access. It does not erase or destroy the record, and some government agencies may still access it for limited legal reasons.

Records may exist in several places: District Court, arresting law enforcement agency, prosecutors office, Minnesota Bureau of Criminal Apprehension/BCA, and sometimes agencies such as DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, DVS, or licensing/background-study agencies. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")

sourceSections.title: Minnesota Expungement Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should screen Minnesota cases through these pathways in order:
Path A - Arrest but No Charges Filed
If the person was arrested but no charges were filed, there may be no court case to expunge. The person may instead request destruction of arrest identification data such as fingerprints, photographs, and identification records under Minn. Stat. 299C.11, if they meet the statutory conditions. This generally applies when the prosecutor declined charges, charges were dismissed before a probable-cause determination, and the person has no felony or gross misdemeanor conviction in the preceding 10 years. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/299C.11")
Wilma should ask:

Were charges ever filed in court, or was this only an arrest?

If only an arrest, route to non-court arrest-record relief, not the standard court expungement petition.
Path B - Mistaken Identity
If the case was dismissed because of mistaken identity, Minnesota has an automatic expungement pathway under 609A.017. Mistaken identity can include identity theft, someone else using the persons identifying information, witness or law-enforcement misidentification, misinformation, or another mistake. If the prosecutor dismisses or moves to dismiss before conviction and states the dismissal is due to mistaken identity, the court must issue an expungement order without a petition. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.017")
Wilma should ask:

Was the case dismissed because the court or prosecutor determined you were not the person involved?

Path C - Automatic Clean Slate Expungement
Minnesota has automatic expungement under 609A.015 for certain records. The BCA identifies potentially eligible cases, notifies the Judicial Branch, and qualifying records are sealed without the person filing a petition unless a court order prohibits sealing or the record is later determined ineligible. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Automatic expungement may apply to certain:

Record Type Basic Automatic Rule
Dismissed/resolved-in-favor cases Some cases qualify automatically after dismissal or favorable resolution.
Diversion or stay of adjudication Some nonfelony cases qualify after successful completion and at least 1 year without new charge/conviction, except petty misdemeanors.
Petty misdemeanor or misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 2 years from discharge.
Gross misdemeanor convictions Some qualify after 3 years from discharge.
Certain felony convictions Some qualify after 4 or 5 years depending on offense type.
Automatic Clean Slate relief does not automatically seal records held by the Commissioners of Children, Youth, and Families; Health; or Human Services. People who need relief affecting those agencies may still need a petition. (

"https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Wilma should say:

Even if your case may qualify for automatic sealing, that may not fully solve background checks involving DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, teacher licensing, or certain government/licensing reviews.

Path D - Cannabis Expungement
Minnesota has special cannabis-related expungement rules.
Certain nonfelony cannabis records may be automatically expunged under 609A.055. The BCA identifies eligible records and seals them without an application, petition, or motion. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.055")
For felony and some other cannabis-related cases, Minnesota created the Cannabis Expungement Board. The Board reviews eligible cases, often anonymously, and determines whether the conviction should be expunged, vacated, dismissed, or resentenced based on changes in Minnesota cannabis law after August 1, 2023. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.06")
The Cannabis Expungement Board has stated that misdemeanor cannabis expungements may already have been processed automatically by the BCA, while felony and certain other cases are being reviewed over a multi-year period. ("https://mn.gov/ceb/impacted-individuals/")
Wilma should ask:

Was the charge related to marijuana, cannabis flower, THC, or a controlled-substance offense that involved cannabis?

Path E - Prosecutor Agreement Without Petition
Under 609A.025, if the prosecutor agrees to sealing for a person eligible under 609A.02, subd. 3, the court may seal the record without a full petition process, unless public-safety interests outweigh the persons interests. The prosecutor must make a good-faith effort to notify victims and give them an opportunity to object. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.025")
Wilma should treat this as a possible attorney/prosecutor-negotiated route, not a default self-help route.
Path F - Petition-Based Expungement
If automatic expungement does not apply, has not happened yet, or does not cover the agencies the person needs, the person may file a petition under 609A.03, if eligible under 609A.02. Minnesota Courts specifically says a petition may be needed where the person wants relief beyond automatic expungement, including records involving DHS/DCYF. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")

3. Petition Eligibility Under Minnesota 609A.02

sourceSections.title: Diversion or Stay of Adjudication
sourceSections.text:
A person may petition after successful completion of diversion or a stay of adjudication if they were not charged with a new crime for at least 1 year after completion. (

"https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.02")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a diversion, deferred prosecution, stay of adjudication, or dismissal after completing conditions?

sourceSections.title: Clean Slate Automatic Expungement Details
sourceSections.text:
Automatic Waiting Periods

Case Type Automatic Waiting Period
Petty misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Misdemeanor 2 years after discharge
Gross misdemeanor 3 years after discharge
Felony fifth-degree controlled substance under 152.025 4 years after discharge
Other qualifying felony 5 years after discharge
These are automatic Clean Slate waiting periods under 609A.015. The person must also have no disqualifying new conviction and must not be charged at the time eligibility is reviewed. (

"https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Misdemeanor Automatic Exclusions
Automatic Clean Slate misdemeanor relief excludes certain offenses, including DWI-related offenses, order-for-protection violations, fifth-degree assault, domestic assault, interference with privacy, harassment restraining order violations, interference with an emergency call, obscene or harassing phone calls, indecent exposure, and domestic abuse no-contact order violations. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Gross Misdemeanor Automatic Exclusions
Automatic gross misdemeanor relief excludes several categories, including certain reckless driving causing serious harm or death, second- and third-degree DWI, order-for-protection violations, criminal vehicular operation, certain assaults, domestic assault, criminal neglect, fifth-degree criminal sexual conduct, malicious punishment of a child, escape from custody, witness tampering, fourth-degree burglary, interference with privacy, harassment/stalking, emergency-call interference, indecent exposure, nonconsensual dissemination of private sexual images, and domestic abuse no-contact order violations. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Felony Automatic Limits
Automatic felony expungement is narrower than petition-based relief. It applies only to qualifying listed felonies and excludes some listed petition-eligible felonies from automatic relief, including third- and fourth-degree controlled-substance possession, escape from civil commitment for mental illness, third-degree burglary other than trespass-based burglary, and certain interference-with-privacy offenses. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609A.015")
Wilma should use this rule:

Automatic eligibility is narrower than petition eligibility. A case may fail automatic Clean Slate screening but still be worth reviewing for petition-based expungement.

sourceSections.title: Petition Process
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Get the Criminal Record
Wilma should guide the user to collect:
Record Source Why It Matters
Minnesota District Court / MCRO Court file number, charges, disposition, sentence, discharge date
BCA criminal history State criminal-history record and fingerprint-linked records
Arresting police/sheriff agency Arrest records may exist outside court
Prosecutors office Charging and dismissal history
Out-of-state/FBI record, if relevant Minnesota court may ask about full criminal history
DVS driving record, if driving-related Court expungement may not seal DVS driving records
Minnesota Courts explains that records may exist with courts, law enforcement, prosecutors, DHS/DCYF, and BCA, and that driving records are separate from criminal court expungement. (

"https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")
Step 2 - Determine Whether a Filing Fee Applies
Minnesota Courts states there is generally no filing fee if the case was resolved in the persons favor. A fee may apply where the person pleaded guilty or was found guilty, even if the conviction was later vacated, and a fee waiver may be available for people who qualify financially. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")
Step 3 - Complete Petition
A Minnesota expungement petition must include detailed information, including names and aliases, date of birth, addresses since the offense, reason for expungement, offense details, court file number, disposition date, rehabilitation steps for convictions, full criminal-charge history, prior diversion/stay/continuance details, and prior expungement/pardon/record-sealing requests. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")
Wilma should collect:
Full legal name and aliases.
Date of birth.
Current address and prior addresses since the offense.
County and court file number.
Arresting agency and prosecutor.
Charge statute numbers and offense levels.
Final disposition.
Sentence discharge date.
Restitution status.
Probation status.
New charges or convictions since discharge.
Employment, housing, school, licensing, immigration, or family reasons for expungement.
Whether DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, PELSB, licensing, or background-study access matters.
Step 4 - Serve Required Agencies
The petitioner must serve the petition on required government agencies. Minnesota law also provides for victim notice in certain cases, and agencies may object or submit information. A hearing cannot be held sooner than 60 days after service; Minnesota Courts public guidance says the process usually takes at least 4 to 6 months, with the hearing scheduled at least 63 days after service. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")
Step 5 - Hearing
Agencies may object. An objection does not automatically mean denial; the judge decides after applying the legal standard and considering the statutory factors. ("https://mncourts.gov/help-topics/criminal-expungement/criminal-expungement-faqs")
Step 6 - Order and Waiting Period
If the court grants expungement, the order is generally stayed for 60 days after filing and during any appeal period. The court administrator sends the order to affected agencies, and agencies may be required to confirm receipt and compliance if requested. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")

sourceSections.title: Court Standard for Petition-Based Expungement
sourceSections.text:
For most conviction-based petitions, expungement is treated as an extraordinary remedy. The petitioner must prove by clear and convincing evidence that the benefit to them is commensurate with the disadvantages to the public and public safety. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")
For cases resolved in favor of the petitioner or certain diversion/stay cases, the court generally grants expungement unless an agency proves that public or public-safety interests outweigh the disadvantages to the petitioner. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")
Courts consider factors such as:
Nature and severity of the offense.
Risk to public safety.
Time since the offense.
Rehabilitation.
Aggravating or mitigating circumstances.
Employment, housing, licensing, or other reasons for expungement.
Full criminal record.
Victim input.
Restitution.
Recommendations from law enforcement, prosecution, corrections, and victims. ("https://www.revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/609a.03")

sourceSections.title: Wilma Eligibility Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand possible Minnesota expungement pathways and gather the information needed for a court petition or automatic-review check. I am not a lawyer, and this is not legal advice. Minnesota expungement usually seals records; it does not destroy them.

Core Questions
1 What county was the case in?
2 Was this only an arrest, or were charges filed in court?
3 Do you have a court file number?
4 What were the charges and statute numbers?
5 Was the offense a petty misdemeanor, misdemeanor, gross misdemeanor, or felony?
6 What was the final outcome?
Dismissed?
Acquitted?
Diversion?
Stay of adjudication?
Conviction?
Vacated?
Cannabis-related?
7 When was the sentence fully discharged?
8 Have you had any new convictions since discharge?
9 Do you have any pending charges now?
10 Was the case related to cannabis, marijuana, THC, or controlled substances?
11 Was there domestic violence, assault, harassment, DWI, no-contact order, OFP, HRO, sex-offense registration, or a victim?
12 Do you need the record sealed for employment, housing, school, licensing, DHS/DCYF/Health background study, teacher licensing, immigration, or firearms rights?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If Arrest Only / No Court Case
Route to:

Possible arrest-record destruction/sealing under 299C.11. This is not the standard court expungement petition.

If Mistaken Identity
Route to:

Potential automatic mistaken-identity expungement under 609A.017. Confirm whether the prosecutor or court dismissal states mistaken identity.

If Cannabis
Route to:

Check automatic nonfelony cannabis expungement under 609A.055 and Cannabis Expungement Board review under 609A.06.

If Resolved in Favor
Route to:

Potential petition under 609A.02, subd. 3(a)(1), or automatic Clean Slate if the record qualifies.

If Diversion / Stay of Adjudication
Route to:

Check whether 1 year has passed after successful completion without a new charge or conviction. Consider automatic Clean Slate or petition.

If Misdemeanor / Petty Misdemeanor Conviction
Route to:

Check whether 2 years have passed since discharge, no new conviction, no pending charges, and whether the offense is excluded from automatic relief.

If Gross Misdemeanor Conviction
Route to:

Check whether 3 years have passed since discharge, no new conviction, no pending charges, and whether the offense is excluded.

If Felony Conviction
Route to:

Do not mark eligible until the exact statute is checked against 609A.02, subd. 3(b), and the waiting period is confirmed.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should escalate to a lawyer or legal clinic if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
Predatory-offender / sex-offense registration.
Immigration or noncitizen status.
DHS, DCYF, Department of Health, childcare, healthcare, vulnerable-adult, or teacher licensing issue.
Firearms rights issue.
Domestic violence, OFP, HRO, DANCO, stalking, harassment, or victim objection risk.
DWI or driving-record issue.
Cannabis felony or Cannabis Expungement Board issue.
Federal or out-of-state conviction.
Pending charge.
Unclear discharge date.
User needs records for immigration before sealing.
Agency objection received.
User wants guaranteed outcome.

13. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

Your record will be erased.

Nobody can ever see it again.

You definitely qualify.

This will remove your FBI record.

This will fix immigration consequences.

This will restore gun rights.

This will seal DHS or licensing records automatically.

You do not need a lawyer
when there are red flags.
Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a Minnesota expungement pathway. The next step is to verify the court record, charge level, final disposition, discharge date, and whether any agency-specific issues apply.
